source: On The Tape / RiskReversal — Fear is the market killer
summary: Bias toward software due to recurring revenue and high ROIC; prefer either deeply cheap on EV/Sales or positive FCF yield
summary_extended: Baker argues he maintains a strong bias toward software investing because recurring revenue and high returns on invested capital make software companies more resilient in an inflationary environment. Within software, he applies a two-part valuation screen: he wants either companies that are deeply cheap on EV/Sales with a high look-through FCF yield, or companies already generating a direct positive FCF yield. He uses the Thoma Bravo acquisition of Anaplan at approximately 12x sales as a private market value anchor, observing that many public software names trade at roughly a 50% discount to that multiple despite having faster growth rates and stronger competitive positions — a dislocation he views as a buying opportunity. The investment implication is to selectively accumulate high-quality SaaS and software names that meet either the deep-value-on-EV/Sales or positive-FCF-yield criteria, treating private equity takeout multiples as a floor for intrinsic value.
detail: Baker maintains a bias toward software investing due to recurring revenue and high returns on invested capital, which help in an inflationary environment. Within software, he wants either (a) really cheap on EV/Sales with high look-through FCF yield, or (b) a direct FCF yield. He references the Thoma Bravo takeout of Anaplan at ~12x sales as a private market value anchor, noting many public software names trade at a 50% discount to that multiple despite faster growth and stronger competitive positions.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I for sure still have a bias toward software' and provides specific valuation criteria. He says he 'at least feels better and has more confidence' with software names at deep discounts to private market value.
quote_fragment: I for sure still have a bias toward software

ticker_audit.reasoning: Anaplan (PLAN) is referenced only as a data point for the Thoma Bravo takeout multiple (~12x sales) used as a private market value anchor. The thesis is a broad software sector framework, not a specific thesis on PLAN itself, and PLAN was already being acquired so it is not an actionable investment. It is not a direct subject or beneficiary of the thesis.
